coredata.dc:title: A selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for bilirubin in human serum based on europium(III) post-functionalized Zr(IV)-Based MOFs
coredata.dc:creator: Xia, Chao
Xu, Yan
Cao, Meng-Meng
Liu, Yun-Peng
Xia, Jin-Feng
Jiang, Dan-Yu
Zhou, Guo-Hong
Xie, Rong-Jun
Zhang, Da-Feng
Li, Hui-Li
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, a kind of Eu(III) post-functionalized Zr(IV)-based metal-organic framework (UiO-66(COOH)2, Zr-MOF: Eu3+) was synthesized and utilized as an independently luminescent probe for sensing bilirubin (BR) in human serum, a biomarker of jaundice hepatitis. It can be served as a turn-off fluorescent switch for BR because its red emission from Eu3+ can be easily quenched by BR through a fluorescent resonant energy transfer (FRET) process between BR and its ligands, and as a result, BR is recognized successfully. Particularly, Zr-MOF: Eu3+ has shown many appealing properties, such as high sensitivity, quick response (less than 1 min), broad response window (0–15 μM), and excellent selectivity. Most importantly, a kind of portable test paper based on Zr-MOF: Eu3+ probe has been developed for directly assessing the level of BR in real human serum and further diagnosing bilirubin-related diseases via visually observing the luminescent color variation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ln-MOFs
Fluorescent sensing
Bio-marker
Clinical diagnosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Luminescence sensing experiments of Zr-MOF: Eu3+ for BR
Photoluminescence study and sensing for BR
Sensing mechanism of Zr-MOF: Eu3+ for BR
Real application assay of Zr-MOF: Eu3+ in human serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The PXRD patterns of the simulated UiO-66 and as-synthesized compounds 1, 1a, 1b, and 1c (a). TEM images of compounds 1 (b) and 1a (c).

Emission (solid, λex = 320 nm) and excitation spectra (dash, λem = 614 nm) of 1a, 1b, and 1c (a). Normalized ligand-based emission bands of compounds 1a, 1b and 1c (b). The CIE chromaticity diagram of 1a under 254 nm excitation (c), and the digital photo of 1a under a 254 nm UV-lamp; ECNU, an acronym of East China Normal University, was written with sample 1a powders (d).

The absorption spectrum of BR and emission spectrum of sample 1a (a). The emission spectra of 1a suspensions containing different concentrations of BR in PBS, insert shows partial enlargement of the blue ligand-centered emission bands (b). The evolution of CIE chromaticity coordinates and luminescent colors (insert) caused by different concentrations of BR (c). The time-dependent PL intensity curve of 1a suspension monitored at 614 nm with a BR concentration of 15 μM (d, λex = 320 nm). PL intensity of 1a at 614 nm versus BR concentration plot (e), as well as the linear relationship between I0/I and BR concentration from 0 to 5 μM (f). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The decay curves of ligand-based emissions with and without 15 μM BR (a), and the schematic diagram of the sensing mechanism of BR by 1a (b).

Luminescence responses of 1a toward BR in the presence of other various serum background components (a) and digital photos of the test papers after dropping the serum containing different concentrations of BR (the test paper labeled as 1–6 corresponds to blank, 0, 10, 20, 40 and 50 μM BR, respectively) under 254 nm UV irradiation (b). The colors of test papers on the left were read by the color scanning program. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Materials for the determination of BR.

head.ce:title: A selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for bilirubin in human serum based on europium(III) post-functionalized Zr(IV)-Based MOFs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Nanophotonics & Advanced Instrument, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Materials Science, East China Normal University, Shanghai, 200062, China

School of Physics and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Transparent Opto-Functional Inorganic Materials, Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai, 201899, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Key Laboratory of Transparent Opto-Functional Inorganic Materials, Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 201899, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, Fujian, 361005, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, Fujian 361005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong, 252000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . School of Materials Science and Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong 252000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, a kind of Eu(III) post-functionalized Zr(IV)-based metal-organic framework (UiO-66(COOH)2, Zr-MOF: Eu3+) was synthesized and utilized as an independently luminescent probe for sensing bilirubin (BR) in human serum, a biomarker of jaundice hepatitis. It can be served as a turn-off fluorescent switch for BR because its red emission from Eu3+ can be easily quenched by BR through a fluorescent resonant energy transfer (FRET) process between BR and its ligands, and as a result, BR is recognized successfully. Particularly, Zr-MOF: Eu3+ has shown many appealing properties, such as high sensitivity, quick response (less than 1 min), broad response window (0–15 μM), and excellent selectivity. Most importantly, a kind of portable test paper based on Zr-MOF: Eu3+ probe has been developed for directly assessing the level of BR in real human serum and further diagnosing bilirubin-related diseases via visually observing the luminescent color variation.

ce:section.ce:para: Bilirubin (BR), a bile pigment of biladiene, is the breakdown product of ferriporphyrin compound [1–5]. Medically, BR is regarded as a biomarker of jaundice hepatitis. In good health, the BR concentration in human serum should be lower than 17.1 μM. However, a high concentration level of >17.1 μM is present under hyperbilirubinemia or jaundice condition (>50 μM), which will cause the hepatargia, jaundice hepatitis, brain damage and even death of newborns [6–12]. In view of these serious health damages, how to accurately and rapidly determine the BR concentration in human serum becomes more and more important.

Luminescent detection plays a very important role in analytical chemistry due to its visualization readout mode, fast response, operational simplicity, and very high sensitivity in a wide range of concentration [2,13–15]. Based on these advantages, the fluorometric method should be the most appropriate technique for the detection of BR. However, only a few fluorescent probes have been developed till now, including Au nanoparticle conjugated human serum albumin (HAS) protein, beta-diketone modified Y2O3:Eu3+ nanosheets, lanthanide complexes, fluorescent molecular modified proteins (BL22P1B11), and polymers. Moreover, they more or less own some shortcomings such as high biotoxicity, complicated preparation process, high cost, low yield, or poor selectivity and stabilization, etc [16–22]. As a kind of newly developed photoactive materials, lanthanide metal-organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) based luminescent probes for chemical sensing exhibit various advantages such as excellent biocompatibility, high fluorescence quantum yield (QY), narrow emission band, large Stokes’ shift, long fluorescent lifetime, and controllable toxicity via selecting bio-friendly ligands [23–28]. Most importantly, Ln-MOFs simultaneously possess double emissive phenomena from organic ligands and Ln3+ ions, which offers more alternative design opportunities in luminescent sensing applications [23]. Nevertheless, up to now, the luminescent probe based on Ln-MOFs for detecting the BR concentration in human serum is still rare and worth exploring. It will open up a new research direction in clinical diagnosis.

Herein, a kind of new Eu(III) post-functionalized Zr(IV)-MOF (UiO-66(COOH)2) compound, Zr-MOF: Eu3+, has been synthesized to qualitatively and quantitatively detect BR in PBS (phosphate buffer saline) and human serum. This research is designed according to the following two reasons: (1) After post-functionalizing, Eu3+ will be well sensitized by [btec]4- (benzenetetracarboxylic acid) ligands in Zr-MOF frameworks, which promotes the occurrence of the ligand-to-metal energy transfer (LMET) process, and as a result, the characteristic red emission at 614 nm of Eu3+ is markedly observed in Zr-MOF: Eu3+; (2) There is a large spectral overlap between the ligand-centered emission of Zr-MOF: Eu3+, and the absorption of the analyte of BR, which leads to an efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process from [btec]4- ligands to BR in this system. In principle, the FRET process will compete with the LMET process to quench the bright red emission from Eu3+ in Zr-MOF: Eu3+. As expected, the as-designed Zr-MOF: Eu3+ has been proven to be a fast, sensitive, convenient, and highly selective sensor for detecting BR. Also, it should have a great potential to act as a powerful diagnostic tool for BR-related diseases in future clinic.

ZrCl4 and Eu(NO3)3⋅6H2O were purchased from Aladdin. H4btec (benzenetetracarboxylic acid) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Bilirubin was purchased from Adamas. EtOH (ethyl alcohol), NaCl, KCl, Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, Glu (glucose), UA (uric acid), Cre (creatinine), AA (ascorbic acid) and Dop (dopamine) were purchased from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd. All the starting materials were AR (analytic reagents) and used without further purification. And the human serum was purchased from Guangzhou Hongyang Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The filter papers were purchased from Hangzhou Fuyang filter paper manufacturer Co., Ltd. The radius and pore diameter of the filter paper are 10.0 cm and 20.0–25.0 μm, respectively. For convenience, they were cut into strips with 4.0 × 1.5 cm for use.

The UiO-66(COOH)2 (named as compound 1) was synthesized according to a previous report [29]. As-prepared compound 1 was finely ground in a mortar with a pestle, and the resultant 300 mg powders were soaked in 60 mL deionized water with different amounts of Eu(NO3)3⋅6H2O (5 mmol, 10 mmol, and 20 mmol). The suspension was continuously stirred for 24 h under a dark environment in order to make Eu3+ completely react with free –COOH groups in compound 1, which is called as the post-functionalized process in this work. The final solid products were obtained by centrifugation, repeatedly washing with 30 mL acetone three times, and drying at 30 °C over night under vacuum. For convenience, the samples doped with different Eu3+ (5, 10, and 20 mmol) were named as 1a, 1b and 1c, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence sensing experiments of Zr-MOF: Eu3+ for BR
ce:section.ce:para: For BR sensing experiments, firstly, the examined solutions with different BR concentrations from 0 to 5 × 10−5 M were prepared by dissolving BR in PBS buffer and serum, respectively, with pH = 7.4 and kept in dark at 5 °C. Subsequently, 6 mg powders of as-prepared 1a were added to the above examined BR solution of 3 mL, which was subjected to ultrasound for 10 min to keep uniform dispersion. Finally, the emission spectra were obtained under an excitation of 320 nm at 5 °C.

30 mg as-fabricated 1a powders were dispersed in 15 mL EtOH. After ultrasound for 10 min, the uniformly dispersed 1a suspension was dropped on a free filter paper and dried at room temperature for 30 min. Repeating the above procedure three times, the test papers were achieved.

The powder X-ray diffraction patterns (PXRD) of products was checked by a D2 X-ray diffraction analysis (Brucker) within the 2-theta range of 5-50°. The morphology and particle size were observed by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL). The element analysis was performed by a TM3000 (Hitachi, Japan) scanning electron microscope (SEM) with a SwiftED 3000 (Oxford) accessory. The chemical bonding were analyzed using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet Instrument Co., Nicolet Nexus 600, USA). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra, fluorescence lifetime, and absolute fluorescence QYs were measured via a fluorescence spectrophotometry (Edinburgh, FLS 920) with an integrating sphere accessory. The absorption spectra were obtained by a UV–Vis spectrophotometry (Hitachi U-3900). The gas adsorption/desorption isotherms for N2 at 77 K were performed on a Tristar 3000 analyzer. Inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) data were collected on a X series 2 inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometer (Thermofisher, Germany).

As seen in Fig. 1 a and b, under a reflux condition the pure phase Zr(IV)-MOF (UiO-66(COOH)2, compound 1) with a uniform particle size of 20–40 nm was successfully synthesized by using ZrCl4 and H4btec as the starting materials. The post-functionalized process by Eu3+ does not influence its crystal structure, particle size, and morphology (Fig. 1c). For UiO-66(COOH)2, the Zr ions are eight-coordinated with O, and six of them cluster together to form the Zr6O4(OH)4 secondary building unit (SBU). According to the lowest energy configuration rule, each Zr6O4(OH)4 SBU consists of four –O and four –OH groups to form a symmetrical spatial structure with a group space [30]. As a result, the 3D framework of compound 1 is constitutive of tetrahedral and octahedral cages, as shown in Fig. S1 [31]. Here, it is worth emphasizing that only two –COOH arms of each H4btec are involved in coordination reaction, and hence, the uncoordinated –COOH (free –COOH) can serve as an open group to coordinate with Eu3+ and sensitize its luminescence as “antenna” in the post-functionalized process. This speculation has been
F m
3 ‾
m
witnessed by FT-IR in Fig. S2. The strong absorption peak centered at 1715 cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching vibration, indicative of the presence of free –COOH in compound 1 [26]. The N2 sorption isotherm after full activation in Fig. S3 shows that compound 1 possesses a BET (Brunauer−Emmett−Teller) surface area of 399.34 m2/g, which is consistent with the previous report [27]. Due to the steric hindrance effect, the existence of free –COOH groups makes sample 1 have a much lower BET value than 1110 m2/g of the parent framework, UiO-66 [34,35].

In order to verify the coordination between Eu3+ and free –COOH after the post-functionalized process, the FT-IR, N2 sorption isotherm and EDS (Energy Dispersive Spectrometer) analyses of as-synthesized samples were carried out. As shown in Fig. S2, the absorption peak of free –COOH centered at 1715 cm−1 gradually becomes weaker with the Eu3+ content increasing from compound 1, 1a, 1b, to 1c, which could be one of the evidences for the reaction between free –COOH and Eu3+. Additionally, a continuous decrease in BET surface area in Fig. S3 and Table S1 provides the second testimony of Eu3+ coordination on the MOF. The lower surface area after doping Eu3+ is attributed to partial blocking of the open pores in 1 by Eu3+ ions. Thirdly, the element-mapping images of compound 1a given in Fig. S4 indicate that Eu3+ homogeneously distributes in Zr-MOF:Eu3+ particles. Its relative content in 1a, 1b, and 1c further was quantified by ICP-MS and obviously increases from 0.029, 0.040, to 0.045 (Table S2). All these results strongly validate that Eu3+, the red emission center, has been successfully introduced into the Zr-MOF framework system through the post-functionalized process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence study and sensing for BR
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, PL properties of H4btec precursor and compound 1 were examined and shown in Fig. S5. Consistent with previous reports [32,33], the H4btec presents an excitation-independent emission phenomenon with a maximum at 335 nm under 300 nm excitation, which comes from π→π* electron transitions of conjugated system (Fig. S5a) [33]. While for compound 1, its PL spectra exhibit a significant red-shift, and the corresponding excitation and emission peaks bathochromic shift to 365 and 430 nm (Fig. S5b), respectively, which should be attributed to the metal-perturbed ligand-centered emission in Zr-MOF framework and also suggests that Zr4+ ions are successfully linked with [btec]4- ligands to form a 3D framework with cavities [36,37]. After introducing Eu3+ into Zr(IV)-MOF, the characteristic sharp emission bands of the coordinated Eu3+ at 579, 590, 614, 651 and 700 nm are obviously observed, which are ascribed to the 5D0→7F J (J = 0, 1, 2, 3 and 4) transitions of Eu3+ [38]. Monitoring the emission wavelength at 614 nm, the excitation spectra exhibit a broad band with a maximum at 320 nm (Fig. 2 a), implying the presence of an efficient [btec]4- ligand-to-Eu energy transfer (ET) process. Here, it should be noted especially that the blue ligand-based emission cantered at 405 nm becomes very weak, and its intensity declines with the content of Eu3+ rising from 1a, 1b, to 1c, strongly indicating the high ET efficiency from the ligand to Eu3+ (Fig. 2b). As a result, under 254 nm excitation, 1a emits a bright red light with a CIE (Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage) coordinate at (0.52, 0.27) (Fig. 2c), and red ECNU letters, an acronym of East China Normal University, are clearly observed by the naked eye under a UV lamp (Fig. 2d). In order to get the apparent and visual luminescent color variation in the following BR detection, the appropriate blue ligand-based emission and ET efficiency are required. PL performances of 1a, 1b and 1c with 5 μM BR shown in Fig. S6 suggest that compounds 1b and 1c are too sensitive to BR. Therefore, compound 1a with the strongest ligand-based emission is the most appropriate as a probe to detect BR for practical purpose.

Excess BR is a danger sign for metabolic disorders because it can accumulate in body organs causing a series of diseases such as jaundice hepatitis and hepatargia. And hence, it is very important to accurately detect BR concentration in human serum to confirm if there is the jaundice hepatitis or other diseases. To be qualified as a fluorescent sensor for BR determination, the stability of Zr-MOF:Eu3+ in the liquid system is a necessary requirement. Therefore, the structural and luminescent stabilities of 1a in PBS were examined and shown in Fig. S7. It can be seen that, after storage in PBS for 1 week, there are no any distinct changes in structure, suggesting that the Zr-MOF 3D framework is very stable in PBS. The stable emission property not only explains the structural stability, but also indicates that Eu3+ ions have no leakage from the frameworks in the PBS solution (Fig. S7b). The excellent stability is consistent with that of previous reports [29] and makes it qualified as a luminescent probe for determining BR in PBS and human serum.

As mentioned in the introduction part, the design idea of the Zr-MOF:Eu3+ fluorescent probe for sensing BR is based on the presence of FRET process between Zr-MOF:Eu3+ and BR. From Fig. 3 a, it can be clearly seen that there is a large spectral overlap from 380 to 510 nm between the ligand-based emission of compound 1a and absorption of BR, which could lead to an effective FRET process [39]. This FRET process will hinder the ligand-to-Eu ET, and as a result, it reduces PL intensity of Eu3+ in Zr-MOF. In order to confirm this design strategy, the sensing behavior of BR by 1a was investigated by adding 1a into the PBS buffer solution with different BR concentrations and examining their PL properties. As shown in Fig. 3b, the red emission at 614 nm of Eu3+ in 1a is markedly suppressed after introducing BR, and its intensity continuously declines with increasing the BR content. At the same time, a discernible quenched phenomenon can be observed for the blue ligand-centered emission when BR is present (insert in Fig.3b) It also provides evidence that a FRET process is the cause of quenching for compound 1a. The direct visual result caused by PL variation after introducing BR is that the emission color of 1a systematically varies from red to pale cyanine under UV excitation (insert in Fig. 3c). The corresponding chromaticity coordinates calculated from the emission spectra shift from (0.52, 0.31) to (0.28, 0.27) as the BR concentration increases from 0 to 15 μM (Fig. 3c). The straightforward change in body color of 1a insures a more intuitive visual experience in clinically detecting BR. Additionally, the fluorescent response rate of 1a to BR is very fast, as can be seen from the time-dependent PL intensity curve of the 614 nm emission peak in Fig. 3d. The emission quench process of Eu3+ by BR in 1a is promptly finished within 1 min, which is much faster than 30 min of commonly used analysis methods in clinic [10]. Extending the contact time to 5 min, the luminescence hardly decreases further and keeps unchanged. Therefore, the designed idea for detecting BR by 1a is preliminarily demonstrated to be feasible, and Zr-MOF:Eu3+ as a “turn-off” luminescent probe for sensing BR should have a great application potential in future clinic.

In order to quantitatively analyze the sensitivity of 1a as a luminescent probe for detecting BR, a series of samples with lower BR concentrations from 0 to 5 μM were supplemented and their emission spectra are recorded in Fig. S8. The dependence of PL intensity for 1a at 614 nm on the BR concentration is plotted in Fig. 3e. A good inversely linear relationship between them is obtained within the BR concentration from 0 to 15 μM. It can be fitted to the following Equation (1) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9814:
(1)
y = − 59901.10 × C
[
B R
]
+ 884146.82
It indicates that 1a can be used as an excellent fluorescent probe for quantitatively determining BR in a large concentration range of 0–15 μM.

To further evaluate the analytical efficiency of Zr-MOF:Eu3+ to BR, the fluorescent quenching efficiency is calculated by Stern−Volmer Equation:
(2)
I
0
/ I =
K
S − V
× C
[ BR ]
+ 1
Where KS−V is the Stern−Volmer constant, C[BR] is the molar concentration of BR analyte, I0 and I represent the emission intensity of 614 nm before and after adding BR, respectively. In this work, although the calibration curve of 1a exhibits an exponential function trend in the whole BR concentration range of 0–15 μM (Fig. S9), a linear correlation can be fitted in the low concentration from 0 to 5 μM with KS−V of 1.01 × 105 mol−1 (Fig.3f). Compared with a value of 8.95 × 104 mol−1 in a previous report [7], the larger Ks-v of 1a indicates a strong quench effect of BR on sensor 1a. According to the Stern−Volmer Equation (2) and linear correlation in Fig. 3f the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of 1a to BR are estimated to be 0.45 μM and 1.5 μM according to 3σ and 10σ rule, respectively [40]. Although the LOD of 1a is not the lowest among those previously reported probes (Table 1 ), it is much lower than the safety content of bilirubin in the human serum. Hence, 1a is enough to satisfy the daily requirements for measuring BR at normal level (<17.1 μM) in serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of Zr-MOF: Eu3+ for BR
ce:section.ce:para: After verifying the excellent detection ability of Zr-MOF:Eu3+ for BR, it is very necessary to further explore its sensing mechanism in detail. As we know, the luminescent quenching of Ln-MOFs can be usually caused by analyte in the following three manners: (1) the collapse of the MOF framework structure; (2) the direct interaction of analyte with Ln3+ emission centers; and (3) the interaction of analyte with ligands in the MOF framework. From Fig. S10, it can be clearly seen that there is no any significant change of structure in 1a after sensing BR, proving that the Zr-MOF framework retains well even in the presence of BR. The stable luminescent lifetime of Eu3+ in three samples with 0, 5, and 15 μM BR indicates that the direct interaction of BR with Eu3+ emission centers is also absent (Fig. S11 and Table S3). Thus, we can conclude that the luminescent quenching in this work must be originated from the interaction between BR and ligands in Zr-MOF. Typically, it consists of two categories: PET (photo-induced electron transfer) and FRET. The occurrence of the PET process is generally based on the energy gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of donor and acceptor, which means that fluorophore (ligands) as the donor must have a higher LUMO energy level than the quenching agent (analyte) as the acceptor [41]. However, the energy-level distribution of BR and [btec]4- ligands calculated by the density functional theory (DFT) based on the B3LYP/6-31G* DFT model indicates that the fluorophore of [btec]4- ligands possesses a lower LUMO energy level (−2.57 eV) than the quenching agent of BR (−2.11 eV), as given in Fig. S12. Thus, the quenching mechanism originating from PET should be excluded in this work.

Based on the above analyses, it can be asserted that the luminescent quenching of 1a caused by the addition of BR in this work must be attributed to FRET process. This mechanism can be further verified by the presence of a large spectral overlap between the ligand-based emission of compound 1a and absorption of BR in Fig. 3a. Moreover, the extent of spectral overlap (J(λ)) determines the efficiency of FRET, which can be quantitatively calculated by the following Equation (3)
(3)
J
( λ )
=
∫
0
∞
F
D
( λ )
ε
A
( λ )
λ
d
4
λ
where F
D (λ) describes the corrected PL intensity of the fluorophore ranging from λ to λ + Δλ, and ε
A (λ) is the extinction coefficient of the quenching agent at λ [42]. According to Equation (3), the calculated J(λ) is 1.69 × 1018 M−1cm−1nm4 in this work, which could efficiently promote the FRET process.

In addition, the occurred FRET process between BR and ligands can be also proven by the variation of fluorescence lifetime of the ligand-based emission with or without BR. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, the decay time of the ligand-based emission shows an apparent decrease with the attendance of BR. According to the previous investigations [43], an index (E) is usually used to characterize the FRET efficiency from donor to acceptor, which can be calculated by
(4)
E = 1 −
τ
D − A
τ
D
where τ
D-A and τ
D are the lifetimes of the attendance and absence of the BR acceptor, respectively. The calculated E is 55.92% in this work, indicative of a relatively high ET efficiency between the probe 1a and BR.

The inherent natures in molecular structure of BR and Zr-MOFs allow for several interactions between them, such as interaction between the free –COOH of BR and unsaturated Zr (IV) sites, the possible hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups of 1a and free –COOH of BR, and the π−π interaction between 1a and the aromatic rings of BR[7,44–46]. The above intermolecular forces will make 1a to be close to BR as much as possible, which finally effectively promotes the FRET process. In order to verify this point, the Förster distance (R
0 ) and the donor−acceptor distance (i.e., the ligand-BR distance (r)), are calculated by Equations (5) and (6), respectively.
(5)
R
=
0
0.211
[
k
2
n
− 4
φ J
(
λ
)
]
1
6
(6)
E =
1
[
1 +
(
r
R
0
)
6
]
where k
2 is the orientation factor, usually k
2 = 2/3; Φ is PLQY of 1a, and was measured to be 0.65% in this work; n is the refractive index of the surrounding medium (≈1.34 in PBS); and J(λ) is the overlap integral of spectra obtained by Equation (3). The R
0 and r are calculated as 76.88 Å and 73.89 Å, respectively, which fall right into the range of 10–100 Å for FRET [47]. This again corroborates the presence of FRET between ligands of 1a and BR.

In summary, all the above-mentioned evidences reveal an efficient FRET process between the ligands of 1a and BR. The strong red emission originates from the LMET process: the excited UV light is absorbed by the ligands of 1a, transferred to Eu3+ emission centers, and finally characteristic emissions of Eu3+ are achieved. However, after adding BR to 1a, the FRET process between the ligands and BR occurs and begins to compete with the LMET process between the ligands and Eu3+. The energy absorbed by the ligand is no longer transmitted to Eu3+. As a result, the bright red emission from Eu3+ in 1a is quenched, BR is measured successfully. The simple sensing mechanism of BR by 1a is described in Fig. 4b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real application assay of Zr-MOF: Eu3+ in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: For practical applications, the selectivity to the sensing objective is a very important parameter to assess the luminescent probe. Therefore, a series of selectivity tests of 1a toward other typical serum biomolecules such as uric acid (UA), creatinine (Cre), glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), Na+, K+, Cl−, and H2PO4 2− were carried out under the same conditions (50 μM in PBS). As shown in Fig. 5 a, those usual biomolecules cannot cause a significant quench to the emission of Eu3+ because their UV–vis absorption spectra do not overlap with the PL spectrum of the probe 1a in the region of 380–510 nm except BR (Fig. S13), demonstrating the high anti-jamming capability and excellent selectivity of 1a for BR. Hence, Zr-MOF: Eu3+ can be used as a highly selective probe for detecting BR without any influence from other background species.

In order to assess the detection accuracy of the 1a based sensor for BR. The spiking recovery experiments were carried out and the results are listed at Table S4. It is exciting that the BR concentrations detected by Zr-MOF: Eu3+ probe are very close to the set value and the results which measured by the diazotization reaction between sulphanilic acid and BR method [10]. As a result, recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) reaches to 98.88–102.50% and 3.58–5.20%, respectively, which demonstrates that as-prepared Zr-MOF: Eu3+ could be used as an excellent sensor for quantitative detection of BR in PBS with high accuracy and reliability.

Based on the excellent selectivity, fast fluorescent response, high accuracy and reliability of 1a to BR, we planned to apply 1a to detect the BR level in human serum for diagnosing jaundice. A kind of test paper was developed by dropping the suspension of 1a on the free filter paper. The PXRD, SEM and EDS (Figs. S14–16) of the as-synthesized test-paper exhibit that 1a is not only successfully embedded into the voids of fibers, but also the original Zr-MOF framework and components are retained very well. As shown in Figs. S17 and S18, the test-paper exhibits the characteristic emission performance of 1a, and is not affected by the background filter paper. While no fluorescence is observed for the free filter paper. When the BR in human serum was detected by the as-prepared test paper, a series of serum samples with different levels of BR were dropped onto the test papers. No. 1–6 corresponds to blank, pure serum, 10, 20, 40, and 50 μM BR, respectively. Their digital images under the exposure of a 254 nm UV lamp after dried in air were recorded and depicted in Fig. 5b. Obviously, the pure human serum does not impact the emission of the probe 1a in the test paper (test papers No. 1 and 2). After introducing BR, the emission color of the test papers changes from bright red to modena and dark navy with the increase of the BR content (test papers No. 3–6). The color variation can be easily witnessed by human naked eye and color scanning APP. Especially, the different emission color represents the different states of human health. The test paper exhibiting the faint red color means that the BR is at a normal level. While, under hyperbilirubinemia or jaundice hepatitis, a modena or dark navy is observed. Therefore, we can definitively conclude that Zr-MOF:Eu3+ synthesized in this work can act as a visually fluorescent probe for simply, directly, and fast recognizing and detecting BR in human serum. In further, its content can also be evaluated quantitatively to help diagnose the related diseases such as hyperbilirubinemia and jaundice hepatitis in clinic.

In conclusion, a kind of novel switch-off fluorescent probe based on Eu(III) post-functionalized Zr(IV)-MOF (UiO-66(COOH)2) was successfully designed to detect BR in human serum. The developed sensor showed a significantly fluorescent quenching effect, high sensitivity (LOD = 0.45 μM, LOQ = 1.5 μM), quick response (less than 1 min), high detection accuracy and broad linear range (0–15 μM) to BR. Moreover, it was not affected by other coexisting species in serum. Finally, a simple and visual method of diagnosing BR-related diseases was developed by designing a kind of portable BR test paper based on Zr-MOF:Eu3+ probe which avoids complex operational skills and professional instruments. Such a fast, sensitive, convenient, and highly selective sensor for detecting BR is anticipated to be a powerful diagnostic tool for BR-related diseases in the future clinic.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interests regarding the publication of this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks are given to the financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51472087), Shanghai Municipal Natural Science Foundation (No. 19ZR1415400), and Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Transparent Opto-functional Inorganic Materials,Chinese Academy of Sciences (No.KLTOIM201606).
